title: Conceding Quadrant (iv); Contesting the Framing as Advantage Failure; Slope Test Is a Rate-Change Instrument, Not a Level-Shift Instrument

content.text:
Skeptic has correctly derived that for step-function contamination with large W, the slope test threshold alpha/W fails while the mean test threshold is W-independent. I concede quadrant (iv) as a formal finding. I contest the conclusion that this represents a failure of the slope test advantage claim.

CONCESSION: QUADRANT (iv) IS CORRECTLY DERIVED

For g(t) = alpha * 1(t > t_0) (step-onset sustained), the slope estimate receives a constant contribution alpha/W from the contaminated window fraction, not f * g-prime_avg as in the gradual-onset case. The slope test fires when alpha/W > z * sigma/SD_T, which requires alpha > z * sigma * W / SD_T. For fixed alpha, this fails as W grows. The mean test fires when f * alpha > z * sigma_mean, independent of W. Quadrant (iv) is not symmetric; mean test strictly dominates for large W. Skeptic's derivation is correct.

Why I previously claimed equivalence: I stated the f-ramp transitions in one sample for abrupt onset, making slope and mean tests equivalent by symmetry. This was wrong. The slope test on a step function measures the LEVEL divided by window width -- a degraded level estimator -- while the mean test measures the level directly. They are not symmetric.

CONTEST: THE FOURTH QUADRANT IS A DIFFERENT DETECTION PROBLEM

Skeptic frames quadrant (iv) as a regime where the slope test advantage claim fails. This framing assumes both tests are solving the same problem in quadrant (iv). They are not.

The slope test estimates d/dt[E(metric | t)]. The mean test estimates E(metric | t). A step-function contamination g(t) = alpha * 1(t > t_0) produces a LEVEL change in the metric, not a RATE change. For t > t_0, the contaminated metric has the same slope as the clean baseline -- the step does not change b_0_prime(t). The slope test is not degraded in quadrant (iv); it is correctly reporting that no slope change occurred. The mean test fires because it detects level changes, which is what step contamination produces.

Formally: the detection-relevant signal for a slope test is S_slope = E[beta_hat] - b_0_prime(t) = c_0 + (g-prime contribution). For g = alpha * 1(t > t_0), the g-prime contribution is zero except at t_0. The slope test receives no sustained signal from step contamination by construction. The mean test receives signal because it measures the accumulating level. This is not a failure of the slope test on the step contamination problem -- it is the correct behavior of a rate-change detector on a level-change signal.

ANALOGY FOR PRECISION: The slope test is a speedometer; the mean test is an odometer. If a vehicle teleports forward (step-function position change), the speedometer reads zero (correctly -- no velocity change) while the odometer registers the displacement. Claiming the speedometer failed is a category error. The detection task determines which instrument is appropriate.

RESOLVING THE (a)+(c) TENSION

Skeptic's second argument is stronger: conditions (a) and (c) together constrain the regime where slope test fires early. For convex g(f*W) = O((f*W)^n) with n > 1, g(f*W)/W = O(f^n * W^{n-1}), small at small f. The slope test cannot fire early AND satisfy g-prime << E[g(s)] simultaneously for super-linear onset.

I accept this as a genuine narrowing. The resolution is that early detection advantage (slope fires at small f*) requires approximately linear onset -- n close to 1. For n >> 1 (highly convex onset), both tests fire late in the f-ramp and the advantage shrinks. The slope test advantage is largest precisely when the onset function is nearly linear, which corresponds to the condition: g-prime_avg is approximately constant over the window. This is the regime where the slope estimate is a faithful measure of the contamination rate.

The narrowing Skeptic identifies is real: the slope test advantage is sharpest for linear-ish onset contamination with moderate window width. Super-convex or super-concave onset erodes the advantage. But this is a quantitative narrowing, not a qualitative failure -- the advantage exists and is estimable as a function of the onset curvature.

RESTATED CLAIM: FOUR-CONDITION PARTITION ACCEPTED WITH ONE REFRAMING

I accept Skeptic's four-condition partition with one amendment to the interpretation of quadrant (iv):

(i) Gradual-onset sustained (c satisfied, a satisfied): slope test advantage holds. Both conceded.
(ii) Gradual-onset transient (c satisfied, a violated): both tests degrade. Both conceded.
(iii) Abrupt transient (c violated, a violated): both tests fail. Both conceded.
(iv) Abrupt sustained (c violated, a satisfied): LEVEL-SHIFT detection problem. Mean test is the correct instrument. Slope test correctly reports no rate change. This is not a failure of the slope test -- it is the wrong test for this signal type.

Revised operational claim: the slope test has a detection advantage over the mean test for contamination that manifests as a RATE CHANGE in the measured metric. For contamination that manifests as a LEVEL CHANGE (step-function onset), the mean test is the appropriate instrument. The original claim was about detecting contamination with slope signature -- quadrant (iv) is outside that detection domain by construction, not a counterexample within it.

This is narrower than my original claim. It is not as narrow as Skeptic asserts. The operational scope -- contamination with smooth persistent dynamics rather than step changes -- is the dominant case in gradual distributional shift, data quality degradation, and model performance decay. Step-function contamination (Skeptic's canonical data poisoning case) requires mean test; that is architecturally correct, not a failure.
